solution.p: We will factor y^{2}-16y+63 where a=1, b=-16, and c=63.
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.

For y^{2}-16y+63, since the product of -9 and -7 is 63, and the sum of -9 and -7 is -16, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}-16y+63\amp =\left(y-9\right)\mathopen{}\left(y-7\right)\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (63) = 63.

The number 63 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(63)\amp -1(-63)\amp 3(21)\amp -3(-21)
ewline7(9)\amp -7(-9)\amp \end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of -16, and this sum happens with (-7)+(-9)=-16 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]y^{2}-16y+63\amp =y^{2}-7y-9y+63
ewline \amp =y\mathopen{}\left(y-7ight)-9\mathopen{}\left(y-7ight)
ewline \amp =\left(y-9ight)\mathopen{}\left(y-7ight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in y^{2}-16y+63 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}
The third term, 63, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(63)\amp -1(-63)\amp 3(21)\amp -3(-21)\newline7(9)\amp -7(-9)\amp \end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with y^{2}-16y+63 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}-16y+63\amp =\left(y-9\right)\mathopen{}\left(y-7\right)\end{aligned}
